title: Elizabeth (Elise) Watt -Hiring Manger Interview | Engagement Manager (Account Management)
meeting_title: Elizabeth (Elise) Watt -Hiring Manger Interview | Engagement Manager (Account Management)

transcript.text: This meeting is being recorded.
Hi.
Howdy.
How you doing?
Good.
Sorry, I was a minute late.
I was in the wrong Zoom account.
Oh, no worries.
I love your background.
It looks very cozy.
Thank you.
I've had a lot of people comment on it, and I'm always like, I'm just manifesting this.
Is that, did you make that with AI?
Uh-huh.
Nice.
Yes, yes.
We actually did put an offer in on a place yesterday.
Yeah, yesterday.
Oh, my gosh.
But there's somebody else who's also going to put an offer in, they think, today.
So, I don't know.
I'm not holding my breath.
Okay.
Dang.
We have had so many of these the last month, like, at least.
Three.
Three other offers which didn't work out.
Dang.
You're having to really channel radical acceptance and non-attachment.
Yes, for sure.
For sure.
I think yesterday when I did it, I was just like, oh, I need to do this because I really want to try and do this.
But then at the same time, I was also like accepting it.
And then for some reason today, was talking to a real estate agent or like texting with her and I started to like really want it again.
And I was like, oh, damn it.
need to do this.
Yeah.
Radical acceptance.
Like you said.
Yeah.
Well, it's a constant effort.
I mean, it's such a huge decision.
And also like to have a property would be transformative in your life.
And so it's hard not to get attached.
Yeah, for sure.
Well, let's get into it.
Yeah.
Cool.
So I'm going to start with like a little bit of a walk.
Thank
Through of the company and of what this is and what the team looks like and all that stuff, it's good just to like set the scene a little bit more.
I know we may have talked about it a little bit, but it's good to like refresh it.
Plus, I think you talked with Marilia like last year.
So it's been a minute.
Yeah.
Okay.
So GrowthX, as you know, similar-ish in some ways to Aerox in that it's, you know, a agentic, pipeline agentic content creation company.
The big difference is that we, well, the technology is completely different, but also we are primarily a services business.
So you bring in clients and you handle everything from top to bottom.
The way that this works is when they bring a client in, they go through this eight-week sprint, which I lead that team.
And then after the eight-week sprint, they go into the larger, they call it the phase two, which is typically a year-long contract.
Which means.
Is that this part of it is really a continuation of the sales process.
It's three things.
It's a continuation of the sales process.
It is you building it from scratch.
And then it's really, in many ways, you introducing or teaching the client about GrowthX.
So there's like an educational element to it.
There's a sales.
I say sales with quotation marks because it's really a soft sale.
It's more you just really doing everything you can to build trust.
With the client and getting them to kind of feel good about them spending all this money with us.
And then internally, it's really building the content strategy.
So it's really probably the most important part of the business.
Because if this part of the business doesn't work, then nothing else works.
They have some pretty, you know, important or not important, but some pretty like stringent goals for how many people we convert.
Or if I flip the script, how many people.
We want to lose as few people as possible once they go through the sprint and then get them into the full thing.
So that kind of gives you the high-level stuff.
And then it's broken out.
The team is about, I think, like maybe 10 people or so.
And it's this team, this sprint-based team.
Just the sprint team, yeah.
So the one I'm leading right now.
So there's like, I want to say like five or six managing editors.
And then there is currently three engagement managers.
Now to make things simpler because you're not used to these words, and it took me a while as well, we're just going to call them account managers and editors, okay?
So account managers, there's three of them, and then there's the editors.
And so the way that this typically works is that the account managers, they interface with the client.
They should be really primarily the only people communicating with the client.
And they do so once a week.
For eight weeks in person, and then they do everything else through Slack.
And so that's kind of the structure there.
And then one of the core things that the account manager does is they also build the content strategy.
Once you've built the content strategy, everything else in terms of execution is the editor.
So then the editor creates the content calendar based off of the content strategy that you got approved.
They will also actually create the content, and they will also put the content into the client's CMS and put it into staging so that the client can then click publish and move forward.
The part that you kind of do in conjunction with the editor is, well, depending on who the editor is, some are much more attuned to this, some are less, but is reporting, right?
So as you're going through this thing, by week, like four or five, more likely five, you're starting to get results, and you want to just be able to immediately start.
Showing the client like, look, we're doing all this stuff.
It's aligned with what you want.
The content is a high quality.
It matches your brand and style and voice and all this stuff.
And we're starting to see results.
We're starting to see impressions, etc.
It's all very, very small amounts.
Like they're not expecting some, you know, like huge thing.
It's content marketing.
But they want to be able to see the results.
The other big thing that is unique about us is that we launched this AI analytics platform.

And so we have our own analytics platform that we then use to like walk people through like basically what prompts are relevant to their business and how they're ranking or how often they're getting cited, what the sentiment is, and a bunch of other stuff.
So your job is basically to do that with between depending on like the period between like six to eight clients that you're going to be managing at one time.
And yeah, and so a lot of your job is is a combination of internal.

work, so working with the editor, making sure that things are going smoothly, and then external work, which is primarily strategy, and then just account management, like just making sure that they have what they need and they feel good, and if they're frustrated about something, you are that person who has to deal with it.
The good news is like, I would say 80 to 90% of the clients are like really excited to work with you, but there are complicated clients.
are clients where the topic itself is like super complex and like very technical, and then it's like, okay, that's challenging to try and hit the right tone.
In some cases, it's that the company is like super big and bureaucratic, and you're having to deal with a lot of different stakeholders and stuff.

So those are some of the types of challenges that you might face, but for the most part, it's like, it's pretty smooth, and in many cases, they really look at you as the expert, as like the strategist.
That's what they're excited about, you know, they're not just selling, we're not just selling.
And like, hey, here's this technology, we're selling the full package.
Any questions?
Yeah, I have a couple of questions.
So when you're, how are the goals being set for the client in this eight-week period?
Like, is that something, like, in this position I would set with the client?
Or, yeah, how does that work?
What does it look like?
Yeah, well, typically the client will tell you.
They'll say, like, hey, so this, in some cases, it's quite broad.
Like, I have a client that said, like, hey, we have had, like at AirDNA, we have had this, like, huge dip in our organic.
Like, we were doing really well, and it was a big part of our business.
And this past year, it just started to plateau, and then I was starting to collapse.
And we believe it's because of LLMs and AI overviews.
And we need to reverse this trend.
So it could be just, like, broad like that.
In some cases, though, they're like, our goal is we're getting 400 leads a month, and we need to get that to 600 leads a month from organic.
And, and
We need to, you know, do X, Y, and Z, right?
So they'll be very specific.
Are you responsible for that goal?

No, you are responsible for getting to set us up for success, meaning that, you know, if they convert to that next stage, hold on, if they convert to the next stage, that the next account manager would be really set up for success, meaning the client feels good and the content strategy is solid and stuff.
You are still building the content strategy.
So if they say, for instance, we want to get our leads from this number to this number, your content strategy would obviously keep that in mind, and you wouldn't do a bunch of tofu content.
You'd be really thinking a lot more about, okay, what's mofu, what's bofu content that we can, like, really, you know, drive towards this and then understanding the audience.
That part is, like, probably the most fun is, like, really thinking through the content strategy, their audience, and all this stuff.
Okay, and as far as, like, time and effort per client, are we generally, you know, saying, well, idea
Produce X amount of content, and it's within a certain range for every single client, or does it vary?

And just for context, like you had said, some of your clients are much more technical, and I imagine like the time and labor to create technical content is going to be different than another company that has something, a more approachable product or something.
So I'm wondering how that's determined.
It's a flat number.
So we tell people we'll get about like 18 to 20 articles by the end of the two months that they're in the sprint.
And so by week four, yeah, week four, we produce three articles.
Week five, it's five, and then it's five moving forward.
Every week, it's five.
So it doesn't matter if it's super technical and it's really difficult, it doesn't matter.
You still have to produce that.
So that's where it can be a bit challenging with the editor.
Okay, that makes sense.
And at the stage where it then gets handed off, like let's say they convert and then it gets handed off to another account director is there.
It's that.
That part of the job is facilitating that transition, like kind of bringing the other account director up to speed, and is there a system in place for that?
In a sense, yeah.
I mean, it's a conversation, and the good thing that growthx does is like it's hyper, like it's all about documentation.
So there's like a ton of documentation that they can rely on.
They also have recordings of every single call.
They can take all those recordings and throw them into cloud and be like, give me all the important things.
But like, typically, it's a conversation between you and the new account manager, and then you hit certain things that you need to share with them.
And then depending on the client, in some cases, you'll overlap a little bit for maybe a week or two weeks.
Or in some cases, it's like super smooth, and you're just like, hey, here's your new account manager.
It was great working with you.
Bye.
Okay.
Gotcha.
Yeah.
Oh, yeah.
And then one more question.
And for as far as types of content, is it just like blog articles, or is it more diversified than that, like downloadable?
Yeah.
Templates, guides, research reports, like what's the scope of content?
Yeah, it's a really important question.
So for us, we just do what they call editorial, which is blog.
And the reason why is, you know, pretty self-evident, you know, in eight weeks, you're not going to be able to do some complex thing.
So we will just do that.
We will mix it up, though, between optimizations and net new.
So we will, in some cases, we will do optimizations versus net new.
But we will not do, you know, PSEO, we'll not do templates or any of those kinds of things or calculators or any of those things in the sprint.
Can that be part of your content strategy, especially if the client's like, I want this?
Absolutely.
Like you can, that can be one of the clusters that you talk to them about.
But it's definitely not something we would do in the sprint.
Okay, got it.
Yeah, cool.
All right.
Let me hit you with my first question.
Yeah.
So my first question is really just trying to understand, like, obviously, if you're dealing with a lot of clients, you have to juggle a lot of different things.
So how do you structure your work week to juggle a lot of different deadlines with different clients?
Like, what processes do you put into place?
What tools do you use to kind of keep yourself organized?
Yeah, I would say that I start with a prioritization framework.
So every account or project is going to have multiple tasks associated with it.
And as far as what the deliverable is, thinking about time and effort, like how much time am I going to need to dedicate to this?
Impact, is this, like, revenue impact for the company?
What kind of steps is it going to move forward towards the goals that I've set with the client?
Also, strategic leverage, is it a one-off task?
Is this a task or project that is going to help me build content the following?
Track work within the context of a tool like that.
Okay, great.
Cool.
Yeah, I will say context switching is definitely a challenge, without a doubt.
So it's good that you're already thinking about that.
Cool.
Okay.
Describe to me a project that you have owned end-to-end that you had to report to leadership about.
But specifically, so that's the context, specifically where something maybe went off track, where it didn't go well.
How did you handle that?
What did you do?
How did you manage the situation, basically?
Yeah.
I have a very recent example of that.
AirDNA's best places to invest report.
So this is, I mean, you're familiar with it, but it's a huge annual piece of content that the company puts out.
There's a lot of different players that contribute to it.
It's, we have a research team that provides the data as the campaign.
I'm both producing the content and then also coordinating with other members of the marketing team to make sure that it gets distributed in the most effective way possible.

I recently had a two-week kind of post-mortem on that piece of content where I was reporting out to leadership, and there had been, for context, like a little bit of disagreement at the start of what the baseline should be that we're comparing it against because it's an annual report and performance has varied over the years on a number of factors.

I mentioned that because when I did finally do that reporting, the year-over-year performance was not what it was expected, and that was, I would say, I would say that would be the challenge or the friction moment there, right, was leadership expecting it to look differently and then having a lot of questions about why that was the case.
So, met that moment, went back, did a deep-dive analysis into what the source of that discrepancy was.
So,
The same page when we're measuring success.
That's good.
And when this first came out and the leadership is like annoyed or whatever it was, like frustrated with the results, I guess, how did you handle that emotionality that's coming out from them?
Were you, yeah, I'll just broadly ask that.
Yeah, I would say I led with like empathy and validating that feeling of being like, yeah, I understand that the numbers aren't what you expect.
I share, you know, I share the desire to like understand this further.
And I also was honest because I didn't have the deeper analysis on hand at that time.

So I, you know, these are the numbers at this point, but, and I can't answer your questions right at this moment, but I'm going to be noting these questions and I'm going to take these questions and let it guide my follow-up analysis on this.
And then just gave them like a really clear understanding of when they can expect more information.
Right.
Nice.
Well, it's cool that the conversion rate was, you know, higher.
mean, that's what you want.
That's ultimately kind of turned a bad thing into a good thing.
Yeah.
Cool.
Okay.
Now, just as I mentioned at the start, like this is a project that, or not a project, a role that does involve like having to work with a lot of different departments, a lot of different people.
So tell me about a project that, and maybe you could use BPTI or something else, but tell me about a project that involved a lot of different departments or specialists.
How did you make sure that everybody had visibility that people were staying on track?
How did you communicate updates?
Like really more the project management side of things.
Yeah.
Yeah.
I will use BPTI as the example again, because that's probably the most like wide ranging campaign.
And that I've managed.
And in those case, stakeholders are, you know, research, different marketing team members, email, social product as well, because we advertise the report within the context of the product.
Asana is definitely like the guiding structure here.
I have a very, like, highly structured kind of SOP that I use within Asana to structure all of the different deliverables, you know, copywriting.
And before data visualizations or before visualizations can be done, the copywriting has to be done.
So it's kind of, you know, this complicated sequence of tasks that are dependent on one another.
A lot of the time with a project like this, I'll start with a ship date and then I'll work backwards, right?
Like, I know that this needs to be published on this day.
Therefore, I need to structure all of the accompanying deadlines for all of the subtasks over, you know, a three or four week period leading up.
So it starts with all of the different stakeholders submitting their needs.
And then I take that information and I infuse structure into it by creating tasks in Asana, creating deadlines, and then also communicating as things change.
An example of that would be with the research team.
This year we produced a lot of breakout rankings for best places to invest by your budget or by a tourism driver.
About maybe halfway or third through the production process, it became clear that we were not going to be able to ship all of the different rankings at the same time.
And then it was a question, it was a conversation with research of being like, okay, well, how do we prioritize which ones we are going to ship?
And as the campaign manager and as someone who's rooted in like SEO strategy, for me, it was like, I went ahead and prioritized them based on impact, like SEO impact.
And then I was like, this is my recommendations for what we should prioritize shipping.
And here's my proposal for how we should stagger the rest.
So that's just an example of like, you know, a project evolving during the course of its execution and how I handled like the communication around that.
Awesome.
Nice.
Okay, great.
Okay, I got one last question.
This is, obviously, this is like an AI company at its core.
So what AI or automation tools have you used?
Can you share anything that you've discovered or built using AI?
Yeah, and then beyond that, as a secondary piece to it, like, what do you feel like is the productivity benefit for you with AI?
Yeah.
I've used a lot of the different LLMs, like ChatGPT being the primary.
I've used Cloud.
I've experimented with Gemini and Perplexity, Notebook LLM, V0.
I've kind of dabbled in a lot.
Mm-hmm.
For a single LLM use, like ChatGPT, I use a lot.
I use their project feature to cluster the different chats that I have around outcomes or subjects, things like that.
As far as building out AI workflows or prompting work, I would say that's mostly been done in the context of AROPS.
And what I've done recently was a schema workflow.
So that's something that AirDNA has not done regularly is build schema for all the different content that gets produced.
Schema is something that's like it's a power agent within AROPS, which means that it's a pre-built workflow.

But I went in the back end and realized through understanding the structure and looking at the prompting that it was very limited scope in terms of the type of content that the power agent could produce schema effectively for.
Um...
Um...
And that it was pretty limited in terms of the scope of the schema.
So I went ahead and reverse engineered it and customized it for AirDNA so that it could create schema for things like calculators for ranked lists and not just blog articles or product pages.
And that's something that's been super helpful.
That's a newer implementation.
So I'm still working on the reporting for that.
But in general, to speak in general to like what the output is or how I measure the success of like AI facilitated work, I think it falls maybe into three categories.
It's like time saved and like productivity increases.
I would say it's output consistency, like the quality of the output over time.
And then the third would be cost.
And that would be from the perspective of like, you know, creating workflows that cut down the amount of time that like a free.
freelancer will have to spend on content on content.
So those are probably the three buckets that I would look at.
Awesome.
What else have you done with, with AeroOps?
Yeah, I have like for BPTI, for instance, I built a workflow that wrote part of the articles.
So BPTI follows like a very formulaic structure where we talk about, we rank locations and there's like a location write up for, for each of them.
So I wrote a workflow that produces the location write ups and so that we could automate the content production for like half of every article and cut down on the amount of time there.
I've also built a workflow that takes, that takes a podcast transcription and, and turns it into an SEO optimized blog article.
I've worked with optimization workflows, taking older pieces of content and refreshing them.
Uh, and then I have also worked.
I on a couple of net new article workflows, one being like a standard SEO article.
There's one that I customized that are blogs to accompany downloadables.

So in the case that there's a piece of content, like a guide or a checklist that the user can download, I wrote a workflow where I provide the guide as the input, and then the AI is able to produce a blog article to accompany that asset.
Very cool.
Okay, nice.
Great.
Those are all my questions.
Do you have any other questions or anything else coming up for you about this?
You spoke to this a little bit, but I'm just curious what the standard client relationship looks like.
Like, are you?
Finding that most folks are using our services as a replacement for in-house content marketing, or are you generally working with a team within the company, or is it just dependent on the account?

That's a really good question because, yeah, I think part of me initially when I started at GrowthX, I would have assumed that it was the first thing, which is like, it's a lot of companies that don't have, you know, content teams, and they're basically like, oh, great, like, I can pay, I could, I could hire like a senior content person, and then they'd hire some freelancers and da-da-da-da-da, and that would cost me like, in total, like, $40,000 a month.
Or I could pay these guys $15,000 and get it going, and it'd be great, right?
Like, it's basically like a replacement in some way.
But what I've learned is that it's really not the case.
I think there's two factors to that.
One is...
In some cases, they have in-house teams, but they have the budget, and they want to do a lot more organically.
so they're like, great, let's just keep these guys, and they're going to be doing this stuff, and then growthx will be doing this stuff.
So it's sort of two parallel lanes.
That's one reason.
And then the other reason, which I think is maybe potentially a bigger reason, it comes up a lot, is they're saying, oh, we don't know how to do this LLM stuff.
Like, how do I create content that ranks for LLM?
Like, I have this SEO or content person that's good, but, like, they don't know how to do this, and I don't want to fire them.
I think they're good at their jobs.
But, like, obviously, we need a solution for this because our business is getting battered down by these changes.
Either that or they're just, like, thinking in the future, they're like, oh, I know this is important, and I need to invest more in it.
I'm oversimplifying.
I think there's probably a lot of other, like, reasons, but it's a...
Combination for sure.
Is it more one than the other?
I think it's maybe like surprisingly, I think it's my quick assessment.
like 60% of them have content people and 40% don't.
But yeah, the 40% that don't, in some cases, it's like, it's like a tiny team and like you're interfacing directly with the CMO.
Okay.

You know, that's your main person as a CMO, which is, which that actually brings its own challenges because then you're dealing with somebody who is like, who has just like so much stuff on their plate.
And that can be, that can be interesting.
Okay.
Yeah.

And for the folks that are, um, where AI site, like showing up in AI citations and increasing their LLM visibility, where that's a major concern, as far as how the content addresses that strategically, like I understand growthx like builds the content with AI, but.
Is the main strategic move there in like the structuring of the content where all of the content that's getting produced is just optimized for like LLM crawlability and citation?
Yeah, that's pretty much it.

I mean, the longer answer to that is like, because we have our own tool, actually the same way that Aerox has their own AI search analytics tool, it allows you to have, hypothetically, I mean, we're still figuring all this stuff out in the long term, but hypothetically allows you to have the ability to be able to say, okay, here's all these prompts that we're interested in, and this one we're not doing very well.
Okay, cool.
It's like immediately address that with content.
And that's something that, you know, I guess in some cases, like if you were to work with freelancers or agencies, they could do that to some degree, but not at all to the same degree.
Right.
Okay.
That makes sense.
Thank you.
Awesome.
Last question I had was thinking about account management.
Like what tools are you, do you all use Asana or do you have a different system?
Like what is your project management?
Yeah, we're actually, we're just shifting.
were using this tool that nobody liked and nobody really used that much.
And we use Notion for everything, like all our tracking and everything.
And we have this incredible person in our team that's like really good at building things.
And so they've built a custom like project management tool for the sprint in Notion.
So it has like a Kanban look in some parts, but it's also, it's, it's still early, like literally we're launching it this week.
So it's still like getting built out, but it's going to have some crazy things where it like pulls directly like calls and puts that into your folders.
And it'll also, it's connected to Slack.
So it'll tell you like, Hey, have you done this thing?
Like.
Like it's, it's going to be very powerful once it's, it's operational.
Wow.
That sounds awesome.
Yeah.
It's, it's pretty cool.
I think it's good.
I think it's going to be really, really powerful once it's like up, up and running.
Nice.
Okay.
And then, sorry, this is, I lied.
This is my last question.
Is how does it work?
Like me working with you?
So I understand how I'm working with managing editors and with the account manager that I'm handing off new clients to, but like, what is, our collaborate, what would our collaboration look like?
My role is, is really primarily I'm responsible.
I'm ultimately responsible for that number that I talked about at the beginning, which is like the conversion rate of the people in the sprint.
So the sprints are eight weeks, right?
So if, if you're, you know, every week, there's some that are coming in and some that are leaving.
And we just want to make sure that that number is, is really stable.
And as a,
Whole the, you know, all the accounts are basically in green, meaning they're all happy and they're very likely to convert.
And so my role for the account managers is escalation.
So if it's like, oh, it looks like this client is unhappy and X, Y, and Z.
And if it gets to a point where it's like, we're really not getting what we need, you would escalate it to me.
Okay.
But broadly speaking, it's, so that's like the very direct use case, but broadly speaking, my role is to manage the whole team.
So I have two weekly calls with all the engagement managers and managing editors all in one room.
And we talk through every single client.
We say, well, what's going on with this client?
What's, you know, what's their status?
What are they feeling?
Where are they at with these tasks?
Have you gotten this done?
Are we delayed?
Why are we delayed?
Et cetera.
Okay.
So I'm just like tracking all those things and making sure it's working.
And so that's, that's my role is I'm responsible for that, that, that thing.
And then I'm overseeing.
You know, how we're doing with each client.
And then there's the escalation part.
The managing editors, technically, so they have a dotted line to me, but they technically report to somebody else.
They report to the chief content officer.
And he is responsible for all of the managing editors that are both in the sprint and outside of the sprint, which is like 60 people.
And so he, if there's an equality issue in the output, he's typically the person that we would, if it's like a really serious problem, he gets escalated to, it gets escalated to him.
We did just promote one of our best managing editors, who's now the lead editor for the sprint.
So now we have two people that we didn't have before where we can escalate things.
The account managers can escalate to me, and then the managing editors can escalate to her when there's an issue with the content quality, and then she will now escalate to the other person.
Okay, got it.
Yeah.
Yeah, I'm giving you a lot of detail, but I don't know.
No, I mean, I've been asking, I have a lot of questions, and it's just such a unique kind of ecosystem to do this.
So, yeah, I'm very curious about it all.
Yeah, we'll just say this, like, I think the interesting thing is we talk a lot about, like, one of the challenges is, in a way, is, like, making sure that people don't think of us as an agency.
But we are sort of an agency right now.
Yeah.
So it's interesting, because we're definitely a hybrid, because there aren't agencies out there that are, like, and we built this, like, custom technology to be able to create your content.
Mm-hmm.
So that's, and we also launched our own analytics tool, like, that's out in the world and, like, starting to attract attention.
So it's very much a technology company with this services layer, but the services layer is super important.
And in many ways, it's a big part of why GrowthX is so successful is because it's saying like, hey, we have this incredible technology and that's in essence what's attracting you to us.
But what keeps you with us and what gets you, what makes you like really like enjoy working with us is because we are also like, we're giving you this service.
Right.
And one of things I've really noticed in the last like, you know, a couple of months that I've been here is that the, that special sauce, which is the, the strategist side can be incredibly powerful.
It can be a big part of why ultimately the client says, oh yeah, I want to, you know, I want to, I want to pay you like hundreds of thousands of dollars for a year long contract.
Right.
And that the strategy piece is also how this position has evolved, right?
Is that the main distinction?
Because previously.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Interviewing Elise Watt for the Engagement Manager role at GrowthX.](https://fathom.video/share/zo78oeyVQbCEqyj5nMw3MxMscQJ2oea2?tab=summary&timestamp=111.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [**The Role:** Engagement Managers (AMs) lead an 8-week sprint for 6–8 clients, building trust and a content strategy to drive conversion to a year-long contract.](https://fathom.video/share/zo78oeyVQbCEqyj5nMw3MxMscQJ2oea2?tab=summary&timestamp=157.0)
  - [**The Challenge:** The core metric is sprint-to-Phase 2 conversion. This requires balancing a fixed output (18–20 articles) with complex clients and technical topics.](https://fathom.video/share/zo78oeyVQbCEqyj5nMw3MxMscQJ2oea2?tab=summary&timestamp=593.0)
  - [**The Candidate:** Elise Watt has direct experience with the core challenge, having managed the complex "Best Places to Invest" report at AirDNA.](https://fathom.video/share/zo78oeyVQbCEqyj5nMw3MxMscQJ2oea2?tab=summary&timestamp=919.0)
  - [**The Fit:** Elise's advanced AI skills (customizing AirDNA's AROPS schema agent) are a strong match for GrowthX's tech-forward model.](https://fathom.video/share/zo78oeyVQbCEqyj5nMw3MxMscQJ2oea2?tab=summary&timestamp=1394.0)

## Topics

### The Challenge: GrowthX's Sprint Model

  - [GrowthX is a hybrid tech/services company; its services layer is the "special sauce" that drives client retention.](https://fathom.video/share/zo78oeyVQbCEqyj5nMw3MxMscQJ2oea2?tab=summary&timestamp=2117.0)
  - [**Sprint Goal:** Convert clients from an 8-week sprint to a year-long "Phase 2" contract.](https://fathom.video/share/zo78oeyVQbCEqyj5nMw3MxMscQJ2oea2?tab=summary&timestamp=163.0)
  - [**AM Responsibilities:**](https://fathom.video/share/zo78oeyVQbCEqyj5nMw3MxMscQJ2oea2?tab=summary&timestamp=285.0)
      - [**Client Interface:** Primary contact for 6–8 clients.](https://fathom.video/share/zo78oeyVQbCEqyj5nMw3MxMscQJ2oea2?tab=summary&timestamp=402.0)
      - [**Strategy:** Build the content strategy (TOFU/MOFU/BOFU mix) to meet client goals.](https://fathom.video/share/zo78oeyVQbCEqyj5nMw3MxMscQJ2oea2?tab=summary&timestamp=567.0)
      - [**Trust Building:** Educate clients and manage expectations.](https://fathom.video/share/zo78oeyVQbCEqyj5nMw3MxMscQJ2oea2?tab=summary&timestamp=184.0)
      - [**Reporting:** Use GrowthX's AI analytics platform to show early results (impressions, sentiment).](https://fathom.video/share/zo78oeyVQbCEqyj5nMw3MxMscQJ2oea2?tab=summary&timestamp=338.0)
  - [**Fixed Output:** 18–20 blog articles per sprint.](https://fathom.video/share/zo78oeyVQbCEqyj5nMw3MxMscQJ2oea2?tab=summary&timestamp=593.0)
      - [**Pacing:** 3 articles (wk 4), then 5/week (wks 5–8).](https://fathom.video/share/zo78oeyVQbCEqyj5nMw3MxMscQJ2oea2?tab=summary&timestamp=622.0)
      - [**Scope:** Editorial content only (new articles & optimizations).](https://fathom.video/share/zo78oeyVQbCEqyj5nMw3MxMscQJ2oea2?tab=summary&timestamp=711.0)

### The Solution: Elise's Experience & Skills

  - [**Project Management (AirDNA's BPTI Report):**](https://fathom.video/share/zo78oeyVQbCEqyj5nMw3MxMscQJ2oea2?tab=summary&timestamp=919.0)
      - [**Process:** Used Asana to manage a complex, multi-department project (Research, Marketing, Product).](https://fathom.video/share/zo78oeyVQbCEqyj5nMw3MxMscQJ2oea2?tab=summary&timestamp=1157.0)
      - [**Method:** Worked backward from the ship date to set all interdependent deadlines.](https://fathom.video/share/zo78oeyVQbCEqyj5nMw3MxMscQJ2oea2?tab=summary&timestamp=1237.0)
      - [**Adaptability:** When the research team couldn't deliver all rankings on time, Elise prioritized them by SEO impact and proposed a staggered release.](https://fathom.video/share/zo78oeyVQbCEqyj5nMw3MxMscQJ2oea2?tab=summary&timestamp=1272.0)
  - [**Leadership Reporting (BPTI Post-Mortem):**](https://fathom.video/share/zo78oeyVQbCEqyj5nMw3MxMscQJ2oea2?tab=summary&timestamp=919.0)
      - [**Problem:** Leadership was frustrated by lower-than-expected year-over-year performance.](https://fathom.video/share/zo78oeyVQbCEqyj5nMw3MxMscQJ2oea2?tab=summary&timestamp=971.0)
      - [**Response:**](https://fathom.video/share/zo78oeyVQbCEqyj5nMw3MxMscQJ2oea2?tab=summary&timestamp=1083.0)
        1.  [**Empathy:** Validated their frustration.](https://fathom.video/share/zo78oeyVQbCEqyj5nMw3MxMscQJ2oea2?tab=summary&timestamp=1083.0)
        2.  [**Honesty:** Acknowledged the lack of an immediate answer.](https://fathom.video/share/zo78oeyVQbCEqyj5nMw3MxMscQJ2oea2?tab=summary&timestamp=1083.0)
        3.  [**Action:** Committed to a deep-dive analysis with a clear timeline.](https://fathom.video/share/zo78oeyVQbCEqyj5nMw3MxMscQJ2oea2?tab=summary&timestamp=1083.0)
      - [**Outcome:** The analysis revealed a higher conversion rate, turning a negative into a positive.](https://fathom.video/share/zo78oeyVQbCEqyj5nMw3MxMscQJ2oea2?tab=summary&timestamp=1145.0)
  - [**Advanced AI Skills:**](https://fathom.video/share/zo78oeyVQbCEqyj5nMw3MxMscQJ2oea2?tab=summary&timestamp=1337.0)
      - [**Custom AROPS Schema Agent:** Reverse-engineered AirDNA's schema agent to support complex content types (calculators, ranked lists), not just standard blogs.](https://fathom.video/share/zo78oeyVQbCEqyj5nMw3MxMscQJ2oea2?tab=summary&timestamp=1394.0)
      - [**Content Automation Workflows:** Built AROPS workflows to:](https://fathom.video/share/zo78oeyVQbCEqyj5nMw3MxMscQJ2oea2?tab=summary&timestamp=1507.0)
          - [Automate formulaic sections of articles.](https://fathom.video/share/zo78oeyVQbCEqyj5nMw3MxMscQJ2oea2?tab=summary&timestamp=1511.0)
          - [Convert podcast transcripts to SEO-optimized blogs.](https://fathom.video/share/zo78oeyVQbCEqyj5nMw3MxMscQJ2oea2?tab=summary&timestamp=1540.0)
          - [Create blog articles from downloadable assets.](https://fathom.video/share/zo78oeyVQbCEqyj5nMw3MxMscQJ2oea2?tab=summary&timestamp=1560.0)

### The Team & Tools

  - [**Team Structure:**](https://fathom.video/share/zo78oeyVQbCEqyj5nMw3MxMscQJ2oea2?tab=summary&timestamp=250.0)
      - [**AMs (3):** Client strategy & interface.](https://fathom.video/share/zo78oeyVQbCEqyj5nMw3MxMscQJ2oea2?tab=summary&timestamp=269.0)
      - [**Editors (5–6):** Content execution (calendars, creation, CMS staging).](https://fathom.video/share/zo78oeyVQbCEqyj5nMw3MxMscQJ2oea2?tab=summary&timestamp=309.0)
  - [**Project Management:**](https://fathom.video/share/zo78oeyVQbCEqyj5nMw3MxMscQJ2oea2?tab=summary&timestamp=1862.0)
      - [**Transition:** Moving from a disliked tool to a custom Notion PM system.](https://fathom.video/share/zo78oeyVQbCEqyj5nMw3MxMscQJ2oea2?tab=summary&timestamp=1862.0)
      - [**New System:** Features Kanban views, call recording integration, and Slack notifications.](https://fathom.video/share/zo78oeyVQbCEqyj5nMw3MxMscQJ2oea2?tab=summary&timestamp=1880.0)
  - [**Reporting Structure:**](https://fathom.video/share/zo78oeyVQbCEqyj5nMw3MxMscQJ2oea2?tab=summary&timestamp=1940.0)
      - [**William Leborgne (Hiring Manager):** Oversees AMs; responsible for sprint conversion rate; handles client escalations.](https://fathom.video/share/zo78oeyVQbCEqyj5nMw3MxMscQJ2oea2?tab=summary&timestamp=1940.0)
      - [**Lead Editor (New Role):** Oversees Editors; handles content quality escalations.](https://fathom.video/share/zo78oeyVQbCEqyj5nMw3MxMscQJ2oea2?tab=summary&timestamp=2045.0)

## Next Steps

  - [**William Leborgne:** Evaluate Elise Watt's candidacy.](https://fathom.video/share/zo78oeyVQbCEqyj5nMw3MxMscQJ2oea2?tab=summary&timestamp=1597.0)
  - [**Elise Watt:** Await feedback on the interview.](https://fathom.video/share/zo78oeyVQbCEqyj5nMw3MxMscQJ2oea2?tab=summary&timestamp=1597.0)
